text.title: Spring Boot Patterns Failure Modes
text.summary: 5 antipatterns observed in real Spring Boot 3 codebases. Each pairs symptom + root cause + concrete fix.

antipattern.symptom: Controller method 200 lines long, computing business rules, formatting dates, hashing passwords.
antipattern.root-cause: Author skipped the service layer "for speed".
antipattern.fix: Move logic into `@Service`; controller stays thin (validate, delegate, return DTO). Enforce a line-of-code budget in CI.

antipattern.symptom: Endpoint leaks `passwordHash`, `deletedAt`, lazy proxy artefacts in the JSON response.
antipattern.root-cause: Same class used as JPA entity and API DTO.
antipattern.fix: Define a Java record DTO; map via MapStruct inside the service. Add a feature test asserting response keys match the expected set.

antipattern.symptom: Some endpoints return `{"error": "..."}`, others `{"message": "..."}`, others 500 with a raw stack trace.
antipattern.root-cause: No `@RestControllerAdvice`; each controller invents its own shape.
antipattern.fix: Add one `@RestControllerAdvice` mapping every domain exception to RFC 7807 `ProblemDetail`.

antipattern.symptom: List endpoint executes 200 SQL queries for 200 results; profile shows one query per row.
antipattern.root-cause: Controller iterates entities and touches lazy associations after the transaction closes.
antipattern.fix: Move iteration inside the service `@Transactional` boundary and use `JOIN FETCH` / `@EntityGraph`. Map to DTO inside the transaction.

antipattern.symptom: Unit test cannot instantiate the bean without booting Spring; refactor that changes the constructor breaks tests.
antipattern.fix: Switch to constructor injection; declare fields `private final`. Add a static-analysis rule (`ArchUnit`) banning `@Autowired` on fields.
